subtopic: Bacterial meningitis workup in the preschool child
stem: A previously well 4-year-old boy is brought to a tertiary paediatric ED by ambulance after 12 hours of escalating fever, headache and vomiting. He is fully immunised on the current Australian schedule including 13vPCV and Hib. On arrival he is photophobic, has neck stiffness on passive flexion, and a positive Kernig's sign. There is no purpuric rash. He becomes briefly drowsy during examination with a GCS of 13 (E3 V4 M6) and his right pupil is noted to be 4 mm and the left 3 mm, both still reactive. Fundoscopy is attempted but the view is limited. His mother reports no recent head injury, no foreign travel and no known sick contacts. He has had no seizures and no recent antibiotics. The ED registrar is preparing to perform an urgent lumbar puncture before commencing antibiotics. The paediatric registrar has been called and IV access has been obtained, with bloods including blood culture, FBC, EUC, CRP, coagulation and venous gas already drawn.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 deg C, HR 142 bpm, RR 28 /min, BP 112/74 mmHg, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: GCS 13, neck stiff, photophobic, anisocoria 4 mm right vs 3 mm left, no purpura, capillary refill 2 s
data_table.Investigations: FBC WCC 22.4, neutrophils 18.6 x 10^9/L, CRP 184 mg/L, lactate 2.6 mmol/L, INR 1.0, platelets 188 x 10^9/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Proceed with lumbar puncture now and start IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg plus IV dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg immediately after CSF is obtained
options.rationale: Reduced GCS with anisocoria is a contraindication to immediate LP because of the risk of tonsillar herniation. CSF collection is deferred and empirical antibiotics must not be delayed waiting for it, per RCH CPG: Meningitis encephalitis.

options.text: Give IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg, defer LP, and arrange urgent neuroimaging only after antibiotics are running
options.rationale: Antibiotics are correct but in confirmed bacterial meningitis dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg q6h should be given just before or with the first dose of antibiotics in children over 3 months because it reduces hearing loss particularly in Hib disease, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Give IV dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg followed immediately by IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg (max 4 g), defer LP, and arrange CT brain after stabilisation
options.rationale: Reduced GCS and focal neurology (anisocoria) are contraindications to immediate LP. Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg should be given just before or with the first antibiotic dose in children over 3 months with suspected bacterial meningitis. Ceftriaxone meningitis dose is 100 mg/kg (max 4 g) load, not the standard 50 mg/kg sepsis dose, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic and RCH CPG: Meningitis encephalitis.

options.text: Give IV benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg alone, defer LP, and observe in resuscitation for 30 minutes
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin monotherapy is inadequate empirical cover in Australian paediatric bacterial meningitis because of pneumococcal penicillin resistance; ceftriaxone (with vancomycin added if pneumococcus suspected) is required, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Perform CT brain first, then LP if the scan is normal, then commence antibiotics once CSF is obtained
options.rationale: Delaying antibiotics until after imaging and LP is associated with worse outcomes. Empirical antibiotics with dexamethasone must be given before imaging when LP is deferred, per RCH CPG: Meningitis encephalitis.

explanation.summary: Suspected bacterial meningitis with reduced GCS, focal neurology, recent seizure or cardiorespiratory instability mandates deferring LP, giving dexamethasone just before or with empirical IV ceftriaxone (meningitis dose 100 mg/kg load, max 4 g), and arranging neuroimaging once stable. Vancomycin is added if pneumococcus is suspected.
key_points: LP contraindications: GCS less than 13, focal neurology, recent seizure, cardiorespiratory compromise, coagulopathy, signs of raised ICP, local skin infection
Ceftriaxone meningitis dose 100 mg/kg load (max 4 g), then 50 mg/kg q12h or 100 mg/kg daily (max 4 g)
Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg q6h x 4 days, given before or with the first antibiotic dose in children over 3 months
Add vancomycin 15 mg/kg if pneumococcal meningitis suspected (Australian resistance)
Notify public health and consider chemoprophylaxis for contacts if meningococcal or Hib
explanation.pearls: Anisocoria with a falling GCS is uncal herniation until proven otherwise; never put a needle in that back.

subtopic: Sepsis evaluation in the febrile young infant
stem: A 5-week-old term male infant is brought to a regional ED by his parents after a single rectal temperature of 38.4 deg C taken because he was unsettled and feeding poorly for the last 8 hours. He was born by normal vaginal delivery at 39+2 weeks with no risk factors for early-onset sepsis: maternal GBS swab negative at 36 weeks, no prolonged rupture of membranes, no intrapartum fever, no chorioamnionitis. He had an unremarkable nursery course and went home on day 2. He is exclusively breastfed and has been gaining weight along the 50th centile. He has had no cough, no diarrhoea, no rash and no apnoeas, and there are no sick contacts. He has not yet received any immunisations as his 6-week appointment is next week. On examination he is mottled peripherally, with a soft anterior fontanelle, no focus of infection, and a capillary refill of 3 seconds. He has been crying weakly but is consolable.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4 deg C rectal, HR 184 bpm, RR 56 /min, SpO2 96% on room air, BP 76/42 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Mottled peripheries, CRT 3 s, anterior fontanelle soft and flat, no focal infection, no rash, weak cry
data_table.History: Term NVD, GBS negative, no maternal fever, exclusively breastfed, not yet immunised

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen after blood, urine and CSF are obtained?

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg alone
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates and very young infants because it displaces bilirubin and is incompatible with calcium-containing fluids. Cefotaxime is preferred in this age group, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: IV ampicillin 50 mg/kg plus IV cefotaxime 50 mg/kg
options.rationale: In febrile infants under 1 month (and many units extend to under 3 months) empirical cover must include Listeria monocytogenes and group B Streptococcus (ampicillin) plus broad Gram-negative and meningitis cover (cefotaxime). Ceftriaxone is avoided in this age group, per RCH CPG: Febrile child and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: IV cefotaxime 50 mg/kg plus IV benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg plus IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin does not cover Listeria adequately as monotherapy here, and triple therapy is not the standard. Australian neonatal sepsis cover is ampicillin (or benzylpenicillin) plus gentamicin, plus cefotaxime when meningitis is possible, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg plus IV gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin plus gentamicin does not cover Listeria, group B Streptococcus or many Gram-negative urinary pathogens adequately and lacks CSF penetration for meningitis cover, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 30 mg/kg and review in 4 hours pending blood results
options.rationale: Any febrile infant under 3 months requires a full septic workup and parenteral empirical antibiotics until cultures are negative at 36 to 48 hours, per RCH CPG: Febrile child.

explanation.summary: Fever in an infant under 3 months mandates a full septic workup (blood, urine via SPA or catheter, CSF) and empirical IV antibiotics. Under 1 month: ampicillin plus cefotaxime (add gentamicin if shocked or suspected UTI). 1 to 3 months: cefotaxime or ceftriaxone, with ampicillin still added if Listeria risk persists per local guideline.
key_points: Listeria and group B Strep mandate ampicillin in young infants
Ceftriaxone avoided in neonates (bilirubin displacement, calcium incompatibility)
CSF should be obtained before antibiotics unless contraindicated
Cefotaxime dose 50 mg/kg q6h for meningitis
Continue antibiotics until cultures negative at 36 to 48 hours
explanation.pearls: If you cannot remember whether to add ampicillin, ask the infant's age in weeks: under 6 weeks always covers Listeria.

subtopic: Identification and public-health response to invasive meningococcal disease
stem: A 7-year-old previously well boy is brought to the ED at 0200 hours with a 14-hour history of fever, lethargy, leg pain and a rash that started 3 hours ago. His mother says he received his 4vMenACWY vaccine at 12 months and a MenB vaccine privately at age 3, but no booster since. He attends a metropolitan primary school and a 9-year-old classmate was hospitalised yesterday with a similar illness. On arrival he is mottled with cold peripheries and a capillary refill of 4 seconds. There is a non-blanching purpuric rash on both shins and the buttocks with several lesions over 4 mm and one early ecchymotic lesion of about 1 cm. He is rousable but irritable, with a GCS of 14, and complains of severe calf pain. The ED registrar gives IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg and a 20 mL/kg fluid bolus. A lumbar puncture is deferred because of his haemodynamic instability and an emerging coagulopathy on the venous gas (lactate 4.2 mmol/L). Blood culture and a meningococcal PCR (EDTA) are obtained. The infectious diseases registrar asks about public health management of close contacts.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.1 deg C, HR 168 bpm, RR 36 /min, BP 86/48 mmHg, SpO2 95% on room air, CRT 4 s
data_table.Examination: Mottled cold peripheries, purpuric rash shins/buttocks, one 1 cm ecchymotic lesion, irritable, GCS 14
data_table.Investigations: WCC 4.2 (neutrophils 1.1) x 10^9/L, platelets 84 x 10^9/L, INR 1.6, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, CRP 96 mg/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate public-health and contact-management action?

options.text: Notify the local public health unit only after the meningococcal PCR returns positive, and prescribe ciprofloxacin only to household contacts
options.rationale: Suspected invasive meningococcal disease is notifiable immediately on clinical suspicion in all Australian jurisdictions; waiting for PCR delays prophylaxis of close contacts, per CDNA Invasive meningococcal disease SoNG.

options.text: Notify public health immediately, give ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally as a single dose to all the boy's school classmates and teachers
options.rationale: Chemoprophylaxis is targeted to close contacts within the 7 days before symptom onset: household and household-like contacts, intimate kissing contacts, childcare contacts (under 6 years) and healthcare workers with unprotected mucosal exposure. School classmates without prolonged close contact do not routinely receive chemoprophylaxis, per CDNA SoNG.

options.text: Notify public health within 5 working days, no chemoprophylaxis needed because he received MenACWY and MenB vaccines
options.rationale: Vaccination of the index case does not negate the need for chemoprophylaxis of close contacts, and meningococcal disease is urgently notifiable (within hours, not days) in Australia, per CDNA SoNG.

options.text: Notify public health immediately on clinical suspicion, and arrange ciprofloxacin 250 mg orally as a single dose (under 12 years) for household and other close contacts in the 7 days before symptom onset
options.rationale: Invasive meningococcal disease is notifiable immediately on clinical suspicion. Chemoprophylaxis (ciprofloxacin 500 mg in adults, 250 mg in children 5 to 12 years, 30 mg/kg up to 125 mg under 5 years; or ceftriaxone IM if pregnant) is given to close contacts within the 7-day exposure window, ideally within 24 hours, as per CDNA Invasive meningococcal disease SoNG and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Notify public health immediately and give rifampicin 600 mg twice daily for 4 days to household contacts
options.rationale: Rifampicin is an alternative but is second-line because of poor compliance with the 2-day twice-daily regimen, drug interactions (including oral contraception), and emergence of rifampicin-resistant meningococci. Single-dose ciprofloxacin is preferred in Australia, per CDNA SoNG.

explanation.summary: Invasive meningococcal disease is an urgent notifiable condition in every Australian state and territory. Close contacts within the 7 days before symptom onset receive single-dose ciprofloxacin (ceftriaxone IM in pregnancy) ideally within 24 hours. Index-case vaccination does not exempt contacts.
key_points: Notify on clinical suspicion (do not wait for PCR or culture)
Close contacts: household, household-like, intimate kissing, childcare under 6 yr, unprotected airway HCW exposure
Ciprofloxacin doses: 500 mg adult, 250 mg child 5 to 12 yr, 30 mg/kg (max 125 mg) under 5 yr
Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM single dose if pregnant or ciprofloxacin contraindicated
Offer MenB and MenACWY vaccination to close contacts if not up to date
explanation.pearls: Gram-negative diplococci on CSF Gram stain plus purpura equals meningococcus; Gram-positive diplococci plus older child equals pneumococcus; Gram-negative coccobacilli in unimmunised child equals Hib.

subtopic: Encephalitis with focal neurology in a school-aged child
stem: A 9-year-old previously well girl presents to the tertiary paediatric ED with 3 days of fever, headache and confusion, and one witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes at home that resolved spontaneously without intervention. Her mother describes intermittent inappropriate laughter and word-finding difficulty for the last 24 hours and a sense that the child has been smelling something burnt that nobody else in the household can smell. There is no rash and no neck stiffness, and she has had no foreign travel, no mosquito-exposed regional travel to northern Australia or to Murray-Darling river settings, and no animal bites or scratches. Her immunisations are up to date for the standard NIP schedule (no Japanese encephalitis vaccine because of no risk exposure recorded). She is taking no regular medications and has no known allergies. On examination she has a low-grade fever, her GCS fluctuates between 13 and 15, she has subtle right-sided pronator drift and expressive dysphasia, and the rest of the cranial nerve and peripheral neurological examinations are normal. There are no purpuric lesions on full skin survey. CT brain is unremarkable. MRI is scheduled for the morning. LP is performed: opening pressure 22 cm H2O, CSF clear, WCC 96 x 10^6/L (90% lymphocytes), protein 0.92 g/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L (paired serum 5.1), Gram stain negative; CSF HSV, VZV, enterovirus and meningococcal PCR are pending, and an autoimmune encephalitis panel including anti-NMDAR antibodies has been sent.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.2 deg C, HR 108 bpm, RR 22 /min, BP 110/70 mmHg, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Fluctuating GCS 13 to 15, expressive dysphasia, right pronator drift, no neck stiffness, no rash
data_table.Investigations: CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis (WCC 96, 90% lymphs), protein 0.92 g/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L, CT brain normal, MRI pending

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management decision?

options.text: Commence IV aciclovir 500 mg/m^2 (approx 20 mg/kg) q8h empirically while awaiting CSF HSV PCR and MRI
options.rationale: Olfactory hallucinations, dysphasia, temporal-lobe-localising features and a lymphocytic CSF in a child are HSV encephalitis until proven otherwise. Empirical IV aciclovir 500 mg/m^2 (about 20 mg/kg under 12 years) q8h must be started immediately because delay increases mortality and morbidity, per RCH CPG: Meningitis encephalitis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Commence IV methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg/day for presumed anti-NMDAR encephalitis pending antibody results
options.rationale: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis is a differential but immunotherapy without first excluding and treating HSV is dangerous because steroids worsen untreated HSV. Empirical aciclovir is mandatory, per RCH CPG: Meningitis encephalitis.

options.text: Commence IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg and dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg, withhold antivirals until PCR results
options.rationale: The CSF is lymphocytic with normal glucose, making bacterial meningitis very unlikely; HSV must be empirically covered with aciclovir without waiting for PCR results, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Arrange urgent EEG and defer all therapy until non-convulsive status epilepticus is excluded
options.rationale: EEG is useful for diagnosis (temporal lobe periodic discharges in HSV, extreme delta brush in anti-NMDAR) but is not a reason to delay empirical aciclovir in suspected encephalitis, per RCH CPG.

options.text: Commence IV ribavirin for suspected Japanese encephalitis given the recent Murray Valley exposure risk
options.rationale: There is no specific antiviral with proven benefit for Japanese encephalitis and no Murray Valley exposure is described. JEV is supportive care only, with vaccination as prevention, per NCIRS Japanese encephalitis vaccine.

explanation.summary: Suspected viral encephalitis with focal features (especially temporal-lobe localisation, olfactory hallucinations, dysphasia) mandates empirical IV aciclovir while CSF HSV PCR and MRI are pursued. Autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDAR) is a key differential, especially in children and young women with prominent psychiatric features, dyskinesias and autonomic instability.
key_points: Aciclovir dose: 500 mg/m^2 (or 20 mg/kg) q8h IV under 12 years; 10 mg/kg q8h adolescents and adults
Maintain hydration and monitor renal function (crystal nephropathy)
MRI: HSV shows temporal lobe T2/FLAIR hyperintensity; anti-NMDAR often normal MRI
EEG: HSV periodic lateralised epileptiform discharges (PLEDs); anti-NMDAR extreme delta brush
Anti-NMDAR triggers include ovarian teratoma; treat with IVIG/plasmapheresis plus steroids
explanation.pearls: Olfactory hallucinations and dysphasia point to the temporal lobe; assume HSV and start aciclovir while you investigate.

subtopic: First-line antibiotic for uncomplicated community-acquired pneumonia
stem: A previously well 5-year-old boy is brought to his GP with a 4-day history of fever, productive cough and reduced activity. He has no chronic illness, is fully immunised on the Australian NIP including 13vPCV, and has had no foreign travel or sick contacts. On examination he is alert and well perfused, with bronchial breath sounds and crackles at the right lower zone. SpO2 is 96% on room air, and he is tolerating oral fluids and his usual food intake at about 75% of normal. There is no respiratory distress at rest, no chest indrawing and no grunting. There is no rash. A chest X-ray performed because of his ongoing fever shows a right lower lobe consolidation without effusion. His mother asks whether he needs to go to hospital.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 deg C, HR 112 bpm, RR 28 /min, SpO2 96% on room air, BP 100/64 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Bronchial breath sounds and crackles right lower zone, no recessions, no grunting, alert and active
data_table.Investigations: CXR right lower lobe consolidation, no effusion

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate first-line antibiotic?

options.text: Oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Macrolides are reserved for suspected atypical pneumonia (Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila), not first-line for typical lobar pneumonia in a young child, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg q12h for 7 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is reserved for aspiration pneumonia or after failure of amoxicillin; the additional clavulanate is unnecessary and increases adverse effects, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg (max 1 g) q8h for 5 days
options.rationale: Streptococcus pneumoniae is the commonest bacterial cause of CAP in this age group. Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg (max 1 g) q8h for 5 days is first-line for uncomplicated CAP in a well child tolerating oral therapy, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic and RCH CPG: Pneumonia.

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg q6h for 7 days
options.rationale: Cefalexin has poorer activity against penicillin-non-susceptible pneumococcus than amoxicillin and is not first-line for CAP, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin 50 mg/kg q6h after admission
options.rationale: IV antibiotics and admission are indicated only if the child cannot tolerate oral therapy, has SpO2 less than 92%, severe respiratory distress, dehydration or complications. This child is well and can be managed as an outpatient, per RCH CPG: Pneumonia.

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated community-acquired pneumonia in a previously well child over 1 month is treated with oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg (max 1 g) q8h for 5 days. Admission and IV antibiotics are reserved for SpO2 under 92%, severe respiratory distress, inability to tolerate oral therapy, dehydration, or complications such as empyema.
key_points: Amoxicillin first-line; covers pneumococcus including penicillin-non-susceptible strains at high dose
Add or substitute macrolide if atypical features (older child, gradual onset, dry cough, headache, extrapulmonary signs)
5-day course non-inferior to longer in mild-moderate CAP
Reassess at 48 to 72 hours if no improvement
CXR not routinely required to diagnose CAP
explanation.pearls: If a well-looking older child gets worse on amoxicillin, think Mycoplasma and add roxithromycin or azithromycin.

stem: A 6-year-old girl with right lower lobe pneumonia diagnosed 5 days ago by her GP has been on oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg q8h but is brought to the tertiary paediatric ED after 48 hours of persistent high fevers, increasing respiratory effort, right-sided pleuritic chest pain and a new reluctance to lie on her left side. She is fully immunised including 13vPCV. On arrival she is febrile to 39.6 deg C, has a respiratory rate of 38 /min with mild subcostal recessions, SpO2 91% on room air, and dullness to percussion with reduced breath sounds over the right lower and mid zones with bronchial breathing above. She is tolerating sips of fluid only and is reluctant to take deep breaths. A chest X-ray shows a right-sided opacification of the lower two-thirds of the hemithorax with blunting of the costophrenic angle and a meniscus sign suggestive of a moderate effusion. Bloods show WCC 24 x 10^9/L (neutrophils 21), CRP 268 mg/L, and a normal lactate. Blood cultures are taken and IV flucloxacillin and cefotaxime are commenced.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.6 deg C, HR 138 bpm, RR 38 /min, SpO2 91% on room air, BP 104/64 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Mild subcostal recessions, dull right base, reduced breath sounds, bronchial breathing above, splinting on right
data_table.Investigations: CXR right effusion two-thirds hemithorax with meniscus, WCC 24 (N 21) x 10^9/L, CRP 268 mg/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation to guide drainage decisions?

options.text: Contrast-enhanced CT chest to assess for necrotising pneumonia or lung abscess
options.rationale: CT is reserved for suspected necrotising pneumonia, abscess, or when ultrasound is inconclusive or operative planning is needed. It is not first-line because of ionising radiation in children, per RCH CPG: Pneumonia.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Pneumonia in children

options.text: Repeat plain chest X-ray in the lateral decubitus position
options.rationale: Lateral decubitus films are largely obsolete; chest ultrasound is more accurate at distinguishing simple from complicated effusion and at identifying septations, per RCH CPG: Pneumonia.

options.text: Diagnostic thoracocentesis only, with pleural fluid pH, LDH, glucose and Gram stain
options.rationale: Diagnostic tap is sometimes performed but in paediatric empyema imaging-guided assessment is required first to determine whether a single drain or fibrinolytics or VATS is needed; pleural pH is also less reliably used in paediatric practice than in adults, per RCH CPG: Pneumonia.

options.text: Bedside chest ultrasound to characterise the effusion (size, septation, loculation) and guide drainage
options.rationale: Chest ultrasound is the first-line imaging in paediatric parapneumonic effusion. It is radiation-free, more sensitive than X-ray for septations, and directly guides whether intercostal drain plus intrapleural fibrinolytics or primary VATS is required, as per RCH CPG: Pneumonia and BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Pneumonia in children

options.text: Bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage
options.rationale: Bronchoscopy is not indicated in a typical parapneumonic effusion. It is reserved for suspected foreign body, atypical organisms in immunocompromised children, or persistent atelectasis, per RCH CPG: Pneumonia.

explanation.summary: Complicated pneumonia in children spans simple parapneumonic effusion, empyema, necrotising pneumonia and lung abscess. Chest ultrasound is the first-line imaging to size and characterise pleural fluid. Effusions with septations or pus on ultrasound, or a thickness over 2 cm with clinical deterioration, are drained, typically with an intercostal catheter plus intrapleural fibrinolytics (alteplase plus dornase) or with primary VATS in selected centres.
key_points: Ultrasound first-line: anechoic equals simple, complex septated equals empyema
Drain if pus, septations, or large effusion with respiratory compromise
Intrapleural fibrinolytics (tPA plus DNase) reduce need for VATS
Necrotising pneumonia and lung abscess seen better on contrast CT
Continue IV antibiotics targeting Streptococcus pneumoniae and Staphylococcus aureus (including community-acquired MRSA if local prevalence)
explanation.pearls: If a previously responding pneumonia stops responding by day 5, scan with ultrasound before reaching for a new antibiotic.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Pneumonia in children

subtopic: Pertussis in the unimmunised young infant
stem: A 9-week-old infant is brought to the ED by her parents after three brief cyanotic episodes at home, the last preceded by a paroxysm of coughing that ended in a deep gasping noise. She has been coryzal for 10 days and her cough has worsened in the last 4 days with occasional post-tussive vomiting. She has not received any immunisations because her parents have been delaying the 6-week schedule. Her mother completed a course of antibiotics 2 weeks ago for a chest infection diagnosed at the GP as pertussis. The infant was born at 38 weeks and her mother did not receive a pertussis vaccine in pregnancy. On examination she is afebrile, has minimal increased work of breathing between paroxysms, and her chest is clear. SpO2 between coughing episodes is 97% on room air but dropped to 78% during the cough observed in ED. There is a single sub-conjunctival haemorrhage. Bloods show WCC 38 x 10^9/L with an absolute lymphocytosis of 28 x 10^9/L. Nasopharyngeal aspirate for Bordetella pertussis PCR has been sent.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, HR 152 bpm, RR 44 /min between paroxysms, SpO2 97% baseline (78% with cough)
data_table.Examination: Sub-conjunctival haemorrhage, paroxysmal cough with inspiratory whoop, otherwise well between episodes
data_table.Investigations: WCC 38 (lymphocytes 28) x 10^9/L, CXR clear, NPA pertussis PCR pending

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate antibiotic regimen for this infant?

options.text: Oral roxithromycin 4 mg/kg q12h for 7 days
options.rationale: Roxithromycin is not the preferred macrolide for infants under 1 month or first-line in pertussis; azithromycin is preferred because it is dosed once daily for 5 days and has better evidence in young infants, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 5 days, admit for cardiorespiratory monitoring and apnoea observation
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months with pertussis are admitted for monitoring because of apnoea and pneumonia risk. Azithromycin 10 mg/kg orally once daily for 5 days is the antibiotic of choice in this age group; it does not alter the established disease course but reduces transmission. Notify public health and offer post-exposure prophylaxis to close contacts, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic and CDNA Pertussis SoNG.

options.text: Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 4 mg/kg trimethoprim q12h for 14 days
options.rationale: TMP-SMX is a second-line option only when macrolides are contraindicated and is not used under 2 months because of kernicterus risk, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone has no role in pertussis; the organism is sensitive to macrolides which are the standard treatment. Ceftriaxone is also avoided in infants under 1 month where applicable, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Withhold antibiotics because antibiotics do not change the clinical course once paroxysms have begun
options.rationale: Although antibiotics commenced beyond 21 days of cough do not alter the clinical course, they are still given within 3 weeks of cough onset to reduce transmission, particularly in infants who are most infectious and at highest risk of severe disease, per CDNA Pertussis SoNG.

explanation.summary: Pertussis in an unimmunised young infant is treated with azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 5 days and admission for apnoea and cyanosis monitoring. Public health notification is required. Close-contact post-exposure prophylaxis (azithromycin) is offered to household and high-risk contacts within 21 days of exposure.
key_points: Azithromycin first-line under 1 month and onwards: 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days
Notify public health on clinical suspicion
Marked lymphocytosis (greater than 20 x 10^9/L) is a classic finding
Maternal pertussis vaccination at 20 to 32 weeks protects young infants
Hospitalised infants need negative-pressure or single-room droplet isolation until 5 days of antibiotics complete
explanation.pearls: An afebrile infant with paroxysmal cough, post-tussive emesis and lymphocytosis is pertussis until proven otherwise.

subtopic: Latent and contact tuberculosis investigation in a young child
stem: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the GP after his father, a recently arrived migrant from a high-tuberculosis-incidence country in South-East Asia, was diagnosed last week with smear-positive cavitating pulmonary tuberculosis sensitive to all first-line drugs on rapid molecular testing. The boy has been well, has no cough, no fevers, no night sweats, no weight loss and has gained weight along the 50th centile on his growth chart. He has not received BCG vaccination as it is not on the routine Australian schedule. He shares a bedroom with his father and has slept in the same bed during the 8 weeks prior to his father's diagnosis. He has no other identifiable contacts of concern, including childcare contacts, who have been screened by the public health unit. On examination he is well, alert and interactive, with no peripheral or cervical lymphadenopathy, no hepatosplenomegaly, and a clear chest on auscultation. There is no erythema nodosum and no phlyctenular conjunctivitis. The local public health unit asks the GP to coordinate his contact assessment. A chest X-ray is performed and is reported as normal. A Mantoux tuberculin skin test using 0.1 mL of 10 TU/mL purified protein derivative is placed in the left forearm and read by the public health nurse at 72 hours, showing an induration of 12 mm with surrounding erythema. HIV serology is non-reactive. The mother asks whether an interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) such as QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus would be more accurate in her son and whether he needs treatment.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 96 bpm, RR 22 /min, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Well, no lymphadenopathy, clear chest, no hepatosplenomegaly
data_table.Investigations: CXR clear, Mantoux 12 mm induration at 72 h, HIV non-reactive, no BCG history

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat the Mantoux test in 8 weeks because a 12 mm result without BCG can represent a non-specific reaction
options.rationale: An induration of 12 mm in a non-BCG vaccinated child with a household smear-positive contact is well above the 10 mm threshold and is treated as latent TB infection without repeating the test, per CDNA Tuberculosis SoNG.

options.text: Reassure that the Mantoux is negative because it is under 15 mm and discharge
options.rationale: In Australia, a Mantoux of 10 mm or more in a non-BCG vaccinated person, or 5 mm or more in HIV or other significant immunosuppression, is considered positive, per CDNA Tuberculosis SoNG.

options.text: Order IGRA (QuantiFERON or T-SPOT) instead because it is more specific and avoid skin testing
options.rationale: IGRA has reduced sensitivity in children under 5 years and is not preferred as a sole test in this age group in Australian guidance; Mantoux remains the test of choice for young children, although IGRA can supplement in BCG-vaccinated children, per CDNA Tuberculosis SoNG.

options.text: Start a 6-month course of rifampicin monotherapy 10 mg/kg daily
options.rationale: Rifampicin monotherapy 4 months is an alternative regimen but the Australian default for paediatric latent TB infection is isoniazid 10 mg/kg daily (max 300 mg) for 6 to 9 months with pyridoxine, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic and CDNA SoNG.

options.text: Diagnose latent TB infection and commence isoniazid 10 mg/kg (max 300 mg) daily for 9 months with pyridoxine, after confirming active TB is excluded clinically and on CXR
options.rationale: A non-BCG child with a household smear-positive contact and Mantoux 10 mm or more, normal CXR and no clinical features of active disease has latent TB infection. The standard Australian paediatric regimen is isoniazid 10 mg/kg (max 300 mg) daily for 9 months with pyridoxine to prevent peripheral neuropathy; isoniazid plus rifampicin for 3 months is an alternative, as per CDNA Tuberculosis SoNG and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

explanation.summary: Paediatric TB contact assessment uses Mantoux as the primary test in children under 5 years (IGRA has reduced sensitivity), with cut-offs of 10 mm or more in non-BCG, 5 mm or more in HIV/immunosuppressed, and 15 mm or more in low-risk BCG-vaccinated. Active disease is excluded clinically and with CXR before starting latent TB treatment.
key_points: Mantoux cut-offs: 5 mm (HIV/immunosuppressed/recent contact in some settings), 10 mm (non-BCG migrant/contact), 15 mm (low risk)
IGRA unreliable under 5 years; not first-line in this age group in Australia
Isoniazid 10 mg/kg (max 300 mg) daily for 9 months with pyridoxine for paediatric LTBI
Alternatives: rifampicin 4 months; isoniazid + rifampicin 3 months; weekly isoniazid + rifapentine 3 months (children 2 yr and over)
Notify the state TB control unit; arrange contact tracing of the entire household
explanation.pearls: In any child under 5 with a household TB contact, treat as if infected even with a negative Mantoux until a follow-up test at 8 to 12 weeks confirms otherwise; the window period matters.

subtopic: HIV testing and post-exposure prophylaxis in the adolescent
stem: A 16-year-old male attends the GP alone requesting a confidential review after an episode of condomless receptive anal intercourse with a new partner 18 hours ago. He says the partner has subsequently disclosed by message that he is HIV positive but has been on antiretroviral therapy with an undetectable viral load for 2 years. The patient is otherwise well, has no symptoms, has had no other recent sexual exposures, is up to date with hepatitis B vaccination, and has not used injecting drugs. He has heard about post-exposure prophylaxis online and asks whether he needs it. He is afraid of his parents finding out. He is articulate, demonstrates clear understanding of confidentiality, his health, and the implications of testing and treatment. He says he is willing to attend the local sexual health clinic but only if his Medicare claim history will not appear on his parents' Medicare statement. There are no concerns about coercion or capacity. He has no penicillin or other drug allergies, and his renal function is normal on a recent school medical.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 76 bpm, BP 124/72 mmHg, RR 14 /min, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Well, no lymphadenopathy, no rash, no mucosal lesions, normal external genitalia
data_table.History: Condomless receptive anal intercourse 18 h ago; partner HIV positive on ART with undetectable VL for 2 yr

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Document Gillick competence, arrange same-day sexual health clinic referral for confidential assessment, and discuss that PEP is not routinely indicated when the source has a confirmed sustained undetectable viral load on ART (U=U), but offer baseline fourth-generation HIV assay, hepatitis B serology, syphilis serology, and follow-up testing at 6 and 12 weeks
options.rationale: The Australian PEP guidelines (ASHM) explicitly state that a known source with a documented sustained undetectable viral load (U=U) is not a recognised indication for PEP because the risk of HIV transmission is effectively zero. Confidential adolescent care under Gillick competence applies, and baseline plus follow-up testing remains appropriate. Confidentiality concerns are addressable via bulk-billed sexual health services that do not generate Medicare statements visible to parents, as per ASHM PEP Guidelines and RACGP Red Book.

options.text: Prescribe a 28-day course of tenofovir-emtricitabine plus dolutegravir empirically now and arrange baseline HIV testing at the GP
options.rationale: Empirical PEP without indication is not appropriate when the source is known to have a sustained undetectable viral load; routine GP prescribing of PEP is also discouraged in favour of specialist sexual health or ED pathways with structured follow-up, per ASHM PEP Guidelines.

options.text: Defer all testing and management until the parents are informed because the patient is a minor
options.rationale: An adolescent who is Gillick competent can consent to confidential HIV testing and treatment without parental involvement; mandatory disclosure to parents is incorrect and breaches the patient's confidentiality, per RACGP Red Book and ASHM HIV Testing Policy.

options.text: Perform an HIV antibody-only test today and reassure that, if negative, he does not need PEP
options.rationale: Antibody-only tests miss the window period; a fourth-generation antigen/antibody assay detects p24 antigen earlier. Baseline testing also does not change the question of whether PEP is indicated for this exposure, per ASHM HIV Testing Policy.

options.text: Inform the patient that PEP is no longer available in Australia and that pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) is the only option for future exposures
options.rationale: PEP remains available across Australia (typically tenofovir-emtricitabine plus a third agent, 28 days, ideally within 72 hours), even though it is not indicated in this specific U=U scenario, per ASHM PEP Guidelines.

explanation.summary: Confidential adolescent sexual health care relies on Gillick competence assessment. PEP (tenofovir-emtricitabine plus a third agent, 28 days) is indicated for significant HIV exposures within 72 hours when the source is HIV positive with detectable or unknown viral load, or unknown HIV status with significant risk. Sustained undetectable viral load on ART (U=U) effectively eliminates transmission risk and PEP is not indicated. Fourth-generation HIV assays are first-line for diagnostic testing.
key_points: Gillick/Fraser competence allows confidential adolescent sexual health care
Fourth-gen HIV assay detects p24 antigen plus antibodies
PEP within 72 h (ideally under 4 h); standard tenofovir-emtricitabine plus dolutegravir or raltegravir for 28 days
U=U (undetectable equals untransmittable) source equals no PEP indication
Baseline testing: HIV, HBV, HCV, syphilis, STI screen; follow-up HIV at 6 and 12 weeks (or earlier with fourth-gen)
explanation.pearls: Confidentiality in adolescent care is preserved by bulk-billed sexual health clinics that don't itemise Medicare statements; tell every teenager who is scared of being seen.

subtopic: Parvovirus B19 complications across patient groups
stem: A 7-year-old girl with homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) is brought to the ED by her mother with 3 days of fatigue, pallor, and increasing breathlessness on exertion. She was diagnosed in infancy and is on hydroxyurea and penicillin V prophylaxis. Two weeks ago her 5-year-old brother developed bright red cheeks with a lacy reticular rash on his arms and trunk after a prodromal coryzal illness; the GP diagnosed slapped cheek syndrome. The patient has had no fever, no chest pain, no abdominal pain and no bone pain. She is fully immunised. On examination she is pale and tachycardic, with no jaundice, no splenomegaly and a clear chest. Bloods show Hb 52 g/L (her usual baseline is 78 g/L), MCV 86 fL, reticulocyte count 4 x 10^9/L (markedly reduced), WCC 6.2 x 10^9/L and platelets 240 x 10^9/L. Bilirubin and LDH are at her usual baseline.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 132 bpm, RR 28 /min, SpO2 98% on room air, BP 96/58 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Pale, no jaundice, no splenomegaly, no chest signs, no bone tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Hb 52 g/L (baseline 78), reticulocytes 4 x 10^9/L (markedly low), MCV 86 fL, bilirubin/LDH at baseline

lead_in: What is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Acute haemolytic crisis from delayed transfusion reaction
options.rationale: Haemolysis would cause reticulocytosis, raised LDH and unconjugated bilirubin. The reticulocyte count is markedly reduced here, indicating aplasia, not haemolysis.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Sickle cell disease

options.text: Splenic sequestration crisis
options.rationale: Splenic sequestration usually presents in younger children before splenic autoinfarction, with rapid splenic enlargement and falling Hb; this child has no splenomegaly and HbSS children typically autoinfarct the spleen by school age.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Sickle cell disease

options.text: Iron-deficiency anaemia secondary to suboptimal nutrition
options.rationale: Iron deficiency is microcytic and develops gradually; her MCV is 86 fL and the drop is acute over days, making this implausible, per BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Sickle cell disease

options.text: Acute chest syndrome of sickle cell disease
options.rationale: Acute chest syndrome presents with fever, chest pain, hypoxia and a new pulmonary infiltrate; she has none of these features and her chest is clear, per BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Sickle cell disease

options.text: Transient aplastic crisis due to parvovirus B19 infection
options.rationale: Parvovirus B19 infects erythroid progenitors and causes a transient red cell aplasia. In sickle cell disease, where red cell lifespan is markedly shortened, this produces a severe drop in Hb with a markedly reduced reticulocyte count (whereas in healthy children the aplasia is clinically silent). The recent slapped-cheek illness in the sibling fits the exposure window, as per BMJ Best Practice and RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease.

explanation.summary: Parvovirus B19 causes erythema infectiosum (slapped cheek) in healthy children, transient aplastic crisis in patients with chronic haemolytic anaemia (e.g. sickle cell, thalassaemia, hereditary spherocytosis), pure red cell aplasia in immunocompromised hosts, and hydrops fetalis or fetal anaemia in pregnant contacts (especially second-trimester exposure).
key_points: Aplastic crisis: severe anaemia with markedly reduced reticulocytes
Treat with red cell transfusion and supportive care
Sickle cell patients are no longer infectious by the time of the rash; isolation is not required from a slapped-cheek child once rash appears
Pregnant contacts: refer for serology and fetal surveillance with ultrasound and middle cerebral artery Doppler
Immunocompromised: IVIG for chronic infection
explanation.pearls: Hb dropping and reticulocytes low equals aplasia, not haemolysis; ask about the sibling with a red face.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Sickle cell disease

stem: A 13-month-old infant has been brought to the GP 60 hours after she shared a paediatric outpatient waiting room for 90 minutes with a child later confirmed by PCR to have measles. She was due to receive her first MMR vaccine at the 12-month visit but it was deferred 2 weeks ago because of an intercurrent febrile illness. Her older sister, aged 4, is in the same household and is fully immunised. Her mother is 22 weeks pregnant and her serological status for measles is unknown. The household also includes her grandmother, aged 68, who was born in 1958 in Australia and has no recorded MMR doses, and an uncle with cystic fibrosis (post-lung-transplant 4 years ago, on tacrolimus and prednisolone). The GP confirms the public health unit has already issued a contact-tracing alert. The infant is currently well, afebrile, and has no rash, no coryza and no conjunctivitis.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, HR 124 bpm, RR 28 /min, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Well, no rash, no Koplik spots, no conjunctivitis, no coryza
data_table.History: Exposure 60 h ago, due first MMR but deferred 2 weeks ago, household includes pregnant mother, unvaccinated grandmother, immunosuppressed transplant uncle

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate post-exposure measure for the index 13-month-old infant?

options.text: Give intramuscular normal human immunoglobulin (NHIG) 0.5 mL/kg now because she is under 9 months
options.rationale: She is over 9 months and so MMR vaccine within 72 hours of exposure is the preferred PEP; IVIG/NHIG is reserved for infants under 9 months, pregnant women without immunity, and significantly immunocompromised contacts, per CDNA Measles SoNG and NCIRS Measles vaccine.

options.text: Administer MMR vaccine now (within 72 hours of exposure), notify public health, and arrange routine follow-up MMR dose at 18 months
options.rationale: For susceptible contacts aged 9 months and over without contraindications, MMR vaccine within 72 hours of first exposure is the preferred post-exposure prophylaxis. If given to an infant aged 9 to 11 months it counts as a dose but the routine 12-month and 18-month doses are still given. Measles is urgently notifiable, as per CDNA Measles SoNG and NCIRS Measles vaccine.

options.text: Withhold any PEP because she is asymptomatic and arrange serology at day 14
options.rationale: Susceptible exposed contacts should be offered PEP (MMR within 72 hours or NHIG/IVIG within 6 days). Waiting for serology delays effective prophylaxis, per CDNA Measles SoNG.

options.text: Administer MMR plus oral ribavirin 15 mg/kg/day for 7 days
options.rationale: There is no specific antiviral with proven benefit for measles; management is supportive plus vitamin A in selected cases, per NCIRS and WHO recommendations.

options.text: Reassure that maternal antibody will protect her until she is 12 months
options.rationale: She is already 13 months old, maternal antibody will have waned, and she is unvaccinated; she is a susceptible contact and needs PEP, per NCIRS.

explanation.summary: Measles post-exposure prophylaxis depends on age, gestation and immune status. Susceptible contacts 9 months and older receive MMR within 72 hours; under 9 months, pregnant non-immune women, and immunocompromised contacts receive NHIG (or IVIG) within 6 days of exposure. Notify the public health unit immediately on suspicion or confirmed exposure. Long-term complications include subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) 7 to 10 years after infection.
key_points: Infectious from 4 days before to 4 days after rash onset
MMR within 72 h of exposure if 9 months or older and no contraindication
NHIG/IVIG within 6 days for: under 9 months, pregnant non-immune, immunocompromised
Notify on clinical suspicion (urgent notification)
Vitamin A reduces complications in severely affected children and in low-vitamin-A settings
explanation.pearls: An MMR dose at 9 to 11 months for outbreak or post-exposure use does not replace the routine 12-month and 18-month doses; document but still give both.

subtopic: Varicella post-exposure prophylaxis in the immunocompromised host
stem: An 8-year-old boy with acute lymphoblastic leukaemia, currently in the consolidation phase of a Berlin-Frankfurt-Munster protocol and on dexamethasone, vincristine and intrathecal methotrexate, is brought to the day oncology unit after a confirmed face-to-face household exposure to his 5-year-old sister, who developed a vesicular varicella rash on her trunk and face this morning. He has not had clinical varicella infection at any time in the past, did not complete the varicella vaccination schedule because of his oncology diagnosis at the time of his routine 18-month appointment, and was last on the day oncology ward yesterday for his scheduled intrathecal chemotherapy. His mother is also concerned about her own immunity because she is 18 weeks pregnant with the family's fourth child, has no history of chickenpox or vaccination herself, and has just had a serology test sent that morning to clarify her immune status. There is also a third sibling, a 12-year-old who has had clinically diagnosed varicella as a preschooler. The patient is currently afebrile and entirely asymptomatic, with no rash, no oral lesions and no respiratory or constitutional symptoms; his most recent full blood count 2 days ago showed a WCC of 1.8 x 10^9/L, neutrophils 0.7 x 10^9/L, lymphocytes 0.9 x 10^9/L, and platelets 84 x 10^9/L. His baseline varicella IgG taken at diagnosis was negative. The exposure occurred 36 hours ago, and the local paediatric oncology consultant is available by phone.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 92 bpm, BP 110/68 mmHg, RR 18 /min, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Well, no rash, no oral lesions, no respiratory distress
data_table.Investigations: WCC 1.8 (neutrophils 0.7) x 10^9/L, platelets 84 x 10^9/L, varicella IgG negative on baseline

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate intervention for this patient?

options.text: Live attenuated varicella vaccine 0.5 mL subcutaneously within 5 days of exposure
options.rationale: Varicella vaccine is a live vaccine and is contraindicated in significantly immunocompromised patients such as those on chemotherapy, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine and Therapeutic Guidelines.

options.text: Oral valaciclovir 20 mg/kg q8h for 7 days starting from day 7 after exposure
options.rationale: Prophylactic oral aciclovir or valaciclovir from day 7 post-exposure is an alternative when VZIG is unavailable, but VZIG (or IVIG if VZIG unavailable) is preferred in significantly immunocompromised contacts. Antivirals also do not replace VZIG in this group when VZIG is available, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine and BMJ Best Practice.

options.text: Oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg q6h for 5 days starting immediately
options.rationale: Immediate aciclovir is treatment of established varicella, not pre-symptomatic prophylaxis; VZIG is the preferred PEP in significantly immunocompromised contacts, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine.

options.text: Observation only and IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg q8h if a rash develops
options.rationale: Observation alone misses the window for passive immunisation. VZIG within 96 hours (up to 10 days per current product information) substantially attenuates disease in significantly immunocompromised contacts. Treatment with IV aciclovir is correct only if disease develops, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine.

options.text: Zoster immunoglobulin (VZIG) intramuscularly as soon as possible, ideally within 96 hours of exposure (and up to 10 days)
options.rationale: Significantly immunocompromised varicella-susceptible contacts (including children on chemotherapy, transplant recipients, and those on high-dose corticosteroids) should receive VZIG as soon as possible after exposure, ideally within 96 hours but up to 10 days. Other indicated groups include non-immune pregnant women and neonates of mothers with peripartum varicella (5 days before to 2 days after delivery), as per NCIRS Varicella vaccine and the Australian Immunisation Handbook.

explanation.summary: VZIG (or IVIG when VZIG unavailable) is given to susceptible high-risk contacts after significant varicella exposure: significantly immunocompromised patients, non-immune pregnant women, and neonates exposed to peripartum maternal varicella. Live varicella vaccine is contraindicated in this group. Maternal varicella before 20 weeks risks congenital varicella syndrome (limb hypoplasia, cicatricial scarring, microcephaly, eye defects); peripartum maternal varicella threatens neonatal varicella with high mortality.
key_points: VZIG ideally within 96 h, up to 10 days from exposure
Varicella vaccine contraindicated in significant immunosuppression
Congenital varicella syndrome risk highest with maternal infection 8 to 20 weeks gestation
Neonatal varicella: maternal rash 5 days before to 2 days after birth equals VZIG to neonate plus aciclovir if symptomatic
Treat established varicella in immunocompromised with IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg q8h
explanation.pearls: If you have to think about whether someone is significantly immunocompromised, they probably are; phone for VZIG and don't wait for the rash.

stem: A 3-year-old previously well, fully immunised girl is brought to her GP with 12 hours of right ear pain and a mild fever. She has been clingy but is eating and drinking normally and there has been no vomiting and no neck stiffness. She has not had ear infections before and has no known risk factors such as cleft palate, immunodeficiency or Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander background. On examination she is alert and consolable, her left tympanic membrane is normal, and her right tympanic membrane is erythematous and bulging with intact membrane and no otorrhoea. She is afebrile in clinic at 37.8 deg C. Her mother asks whether she needs antibiotics or whether she can be managed with analgesia alone.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8 deg C, HR 110 bpm, RR 24 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 100/62 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Right TM erythematous and bulging, intact, no otorrhoea, left TM normal, no mastoid tenderness, no neck stiffness
data_table.History: First episode, fully immunised, no high-risk background, eating/drinking normally

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Analgesia (paracetamol +/- ibuprofen) with watchful waiting and review at 48 to 72 hours if not improving
options.rationale: In a well, low-risk child over 2 years with unilateral acute otitis media, intact tympanic membrane and no severe systemic symptoms, watchful waiting with analgesia is recommended. Antibiotics are reserved for children under 6 months always, under 2 years with bilateral disease, otorrhoea, severe symptoms, or high-risk groups (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children, immunocompromised, cochlear implants), as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic and RCH CPG: Acute otitis media.

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg q8h for 5 days
options.rationale: The Australian dose for AOM when antibiotics are indicated is amoxicillin 30 mg/kg (max 1 g) q8h for 5 days (or 90 mg/kg/day divided), not 15 mg/kg, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5/3.2 mg/kg q12h for 7 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is reserved for failure of first-line amoxicillin, recent amoxicillin use, or recurrent AOM. It is not first-line for an uncomplicated first episode, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Oral cefaclor 10 mg/kg q8h for 7 days
options.rationale: Cefaclor is poorly active against penicillin-non-susceptible pneumococcus and is associated with serum-sickness-like reactions; it is not first-line, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Topical ciprofloxacin 0.3% ear drops 5 drops twice daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Topical ciprofloxacin is for chronic suppurative otitis media or acute otitis media with discharge through a perforation or grommet, not intact-membrane AOM, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

explanation.summary: Most AOM in well low-risk Australian children resolves without antibiotics. Indications for immediate antibiotics: under 6 months, under 2 years with bilateral disease, otorrhoea (perforated or grommet), severe systemic symptoms, immunocompromised, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander background, cochlear implants, or failure to improve at 48 to 72 hours. First-line amoxicillin 30 mg/kg (max 1 g) q8h for 5 days; amoxicillin-clavulanate for failure or recurrence.
key_points: Analgesia is the priority regardless of antibiotic decision
Antibiotic indications: under 6 mo always; under 2 yr bilateral; otorrhoea; severe; high risk
First-line: amoxicillin 30 mg/kg q8h x 5 days
Topical antibiotics for perforation or grommet discharge
Review at 48 to 72 h; reconsider antibiotics if no improvement
explanation.pearls: In remote and Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children, AOM is treated more aggressively because of the high burden of chronic suppurative otitis media; the threshold for antibiotics is much lower.

stem: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the ED with 24 hours of progressive right eyelid swelling and redness on a background of 4 days of purulent nasal discharge and intermittent facial pain. He is fully immunised including 13vPCV and Hib. He has had a temperature of up to 39.2 deg C at home. On examination the right upper and lower eyelids are warm, erythematous and tense; the eye itself is closed by swelling but can be opened with gentle effort. The conjunctiva is mildly injected. He reports pain when asked to move the eye and has restricted upgaze and lateral gaze on the right with a sense of pressure. Visual acuity in the affected eye is reduced to 6/18 from a documented 6/6 at school screening 4 months ago, and there is a relative afferent pupillary defect on swinging-flashlight testing. He has no proptosis measured by Hertel exophthalmometry. The left eye is normal. He has no neck stiffness and no focal neurology.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.1 deg C, HR 128 bpm, RR 24 /min, BP 108/66 mmHg, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Right eyelid swelling, painful EOM with restricted upgaze and lateral gaze, RAPD, VA 6/18 right vs 6/6 left, no proptosis on Hertel
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18 x 10^9/L (neutrophils 15), CRP 142 mg/L, blood culture taken

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg q6h for 7 days and review by GP in 48 hours
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics and outpatient management are appropriate for uncomplicated periorbital (preseptal) cellulitis without signs of orbital involvement. Painful eye movements, ophthalmoplegia, reduced visual acuity and RAPD indicate orbital (postseptal) cellulitis, per RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis

options.text: IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg q6h monotherapy and ophthalmology review in the morning
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin monotherapy does not cover the sinus pathogens (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Streptococcus anginosus group, anaerobes) and Haemophilus influenzae that drive orbital cellulitis. Broader cover plus anaerobic cover is required, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: MRI orbits in the morning before any antibiotics, to confirm the diagnosis
options.rationale: Imaging is required, but antibiotics must not be delayed; CT sinuses and orbits with contrast is the standard urgent imaging, not MRI, because it is more rapidly available and accurately identifies subperiosteal abscess, per RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis

options.text: Urgent CT sinuses and orbits with contrast, admit, IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily plus IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg q6h plus IV metronidazole 12.5 mg/kg q8h, urgent ophthalmology and ENT review
options.rationale: This child has features of orbital (postseptal) cellulitis: painful eye movements, ophthalmoplegia, reduced visual acuity and RAPD. Urgent CT sinuses and orbits with contrast identifies subperiosteal abscess and sinus disease. Empirical IV therapy must cover sinus pathogens (ceftriaxone), Staphylococcus aureus (flucloxacillin) and anaerobes (metronidazole), with urgent ophthalmology and ENT input for possible surgical drainage, as per RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis

options.text: Single dose of oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg and observation, on the assumption of allergic angioedema
options.rationale: Allergic eyelid swelling is bilateral, itchy and non-tender, and does not affect visual acuity or eye movements; this presentation is bacterial orbital cellulitis, per RCH CPG.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis

explanation.summary: Periorbital (preseptal) cellulitis is anterior to the orbital septum and presents with eyelid swelling and erythema but normal eye movements, vision and pupil response. Orbital (postseptal) cellulitis includes proptosis, painful eye movements, ophthalmoplegia, reduced visual acuity, or RAPD and demands urgent CT sinuses and orbits, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics covering sinus organisms, anaerobes and Staphylococcus aureus, and joint ophthalmology and ENT input for possible drainage of subperiosteal abscess.
key_points: Preseptal: lid swelling, no proptosis, normal EOM, normal vision
Postseptal: any of proptosis, painful EOM, ophthalmoplegia, reduced VA, RAPD
Imaging: CT sinuses and orbits with contrast (not MRI first-line for speed)
Empirical IV: ceftriaxone + flucloxacillin + metronidazole
Drain subperiosteal abscess if large, vision-threatening or non-responsive to 48 h IV
explanation.pearls: Always test eye movements, visual acuity and the pupil in any swollen eyelid; a missed orbital cellulitis can blind a child in 24 hours.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis

subtopic: Febrile neutropenia with septic shock in the immunocompromised child
stem: A 6-year-old girl with B-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukaemia is brought to a tertiary paediatric ED by her parents on day 10 of consolidation chemotherapy with high-dose methotrexate and cytarabine. She has a tunnelled Hickman line in situ for 4 months, has been febrile for 4 hours with a single reading of 38.6 deg C documented at home by her parents, has had new rigors over the last 30 minutes, and her parents report increasingly rapid breathing and that she has been progressively more lethargic over the last hour with reduced eye contact. On arrival to the resuscitation bay she is mottled with cold peripheries and a capillary refill of 5 seconds, BP 78/40 mmHg, HR 162 bpm, RR 40 /min, SpO2 94% on 4 L/min nasal prongs, T 39.4 deg C. There is no obvious focus of infection on rapid head-to-toe examination, the Hickman exit site is clean and not erythematous, her chest is clear, and there is no abdominal tenderness or perianal lesion. Her full blood count two days ago showed neutrophils 0.2 x 10^9/L, lymphocytes 0.4 x 10^9/L, haemoglobin 92 g/L, and platelets 28 x 10^9/L. She has no penicillin or other drug allergy, no previous resistant organisms colonising her central line or stool surveillance, and her last echocardiogram performed 3 months ago for routine anthracycline monitoring showed normal LV function and a normal ejection fraction of 64%. There is no history of mucositis bleeding, no recent abdominal pain and no diarrhoea. A repeat FBC, blood cultures drawn from both Hickman lumens and a peripheral site simultaneously, venous lactate, VBG, EUC, LFTs, coagulation, group and screen, urinalysis and a portable CXR are being arranged in parallel with resuscitation. The on-call oncology consultant and the paediatric intensive care registrar have been contacted and are aware.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 deg C, HR 162 bpm, RR 40 /min, BP 78/40 mmHg, SpO2 94% on 4 L/min, CRT 5 s
data_table.Examination: Mottled cold peripheries, lethargic, no rash, no mucositis bleeding, Hickman line site clean, chest clear
data_table.Investigations: Recent neutrophils 0.2 x 10^9/L, platelets 28 x 10^9/L, lactate pending, no known resistant organisms

lead_in: After 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus is initiated, what is the single most appropriate empirical antimicrobial regimen?

options.text: IV cefepime 50 mg/kg q8h monotherapy
options.rationale: Cefepime monotherapy is acceptable for stable febrile neutropenia per some guidelines but the patient is in septic shock with hypotension and prolonged neutropenia. In Australian paediatric practice, piperacillin-tazobactam plus an aminoglycoside (or vancomycin if line infection suspected) is preferred for haemodynamically unstable febrile neutropenia, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily plus IV vancomycin 15 mg/kg q6h
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone does not cover Pseudomonas aeruginosa, which is a critical pathogen in febrile neutropenia. Antipseudomonal cover (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or meropenem) is mandatory, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia.

options.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam 100 mg/kg (piperacillin component) q6h plus IV gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg single daily dose, with addition of IV vancomycin 15 mg/kg if line infection suspected or shock persists
options.rationale: High-risk febrile neutropenia with septic shock and an indwelling Hickman line in a profoundly neutropenic patient requires antipseudomonal beta-lactam plus aminoglycoside for synergy and Gram-negative cover. Piperacillin-tazobactam 100 mg/kg (piperacillin component) q6h plus gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg once daily is standard, with vancomycin added if suspected central line infection, mucositis, MRSA risk, or ongoing instability, as per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: IV meropenem 20 mg/kg q8h monotherapy
options.rationale: Meropenem is reserved for second-line use when there is known ESBL colonisation, severe sepsis not responding to first-line therapy, or significant beta-lactam allergy; routine first-line use risks selecting carbapenem-resistant organisms, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Delay antibiotics until blood cultures return positive to ensure organism-directed therapy
options.rationale: Antibiotics must be administered within 60 minutes (ideally within 30 minutes for septic shock) in suspected febrile neutropenia. Each hour of delay increases mortality, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia.

explanation.summary: Febrile neutropenia is fever 38.0 deg C or more (single reading 38.5 deg C or sustained 38.0 deg C) with ANC under 0.5 x 10^9/L (or expected to fall). Empirical antibiotics within 60 minutes (within 30 minutes if septic shock). High-risk or haemodynamically unstable patients: piperacillin-tazobactam plus aminoglycoside; add vancomycin if line infection, MRSA risk, mucositis, or shock. Source control includes line removal if persistent positive cultures.
key_points: ANC under 0.5 x 10^9/L defines neutropenia
Antibiotics within 60 min (30 min if shocked)
First-line high-risk: piperacillin-tazobactam + gentamicin (+/- vancomycin)
Add antifungal cover if persistent fever beyond 96 h
G-CSF for prolonged neutropenia; cannot replace antibiotics in acute phase
explanation.pearls: Time to antibiotics is the single most modifiable predictor of mortality in paediatric febrile neutropenia; the line must be in and antibiotics running before the imaging trolley arrives.
